environment.hardware: No special hardware required. Verified on Apple Silicon (aarch64) macOS 25.5 (Darwin), no GPU/cluster/large-memory dependency: the Haskell suite operates on small finite algebraic structures (exponent-vector monoid elements over a 3-letter alphabet, an 8x10 lookup table), and the LaTeX build is a standard single-machine compile.
environment.software: GHC 9.10.1 (pinned by cabal.project's `with-compiler: ghc-9.10.1`, reachable via ghcup at ~/.ghcup/bin/ghc-9.10.1; the ambient default toolchain on PATH was GHC 9.14.1 with Homebrew cabal-install 3.16.1.0) with cabal-install for the Haskell suite; pdfTeX (TeX Live 2026) plus BibTeX 0.99e for the paper build.
dependencies: base >=4 && <5 (bundled with GHC, declared in the .cabal file)
containers (Data.Map.Strict; declared in the .cabal file with no version bound; resolved from the local package index/store)
QuickCheck (declared in the .cabal file for the test-suite component only, no version bound; resolved from the local package index/store, no network fetch observed in my session)

concerns: Building the QuickCheck test-suite (test:properties) under the literally-requested flags -Wall -Wextra -Werror fails to compile: Properties.hs:30, :36, and :39 declare `instance Arbitrary Elt`, `instance Arbitrary GElt`, and `instance Arbitrary AZClass`, each an orphan instance (Arbitrary lives in the external QuickCheck package; Elt/GElt are defined in Monoid.hs and AZClass in TenfoldWay.hs, neither of which is Properties.hs). I confirmed with an isolated minimal repro that -Worphans is already enabled by -Wall alone under both GHC 9.10.1 (the pinned version) and GHC 9.14.1 (the ambient default), so adding -Werror turns these three advisories into hard errors independent of -Wextra. Under -Wall -Wextra without -Werror the identical build succeeds with exactly these 3 warnings and nothing else, and all 21 QuickCheck properties then pass, reproducibly, across repeated runs (verified 3x), exiting 0 every time. The main executable target (exe:lattice-eft: Monoid.hs + TenfoldWay.hs + Main.hs) compiles and links with zero warnings under the full -Wall -Wextra -Werror and also runs to completion with exit 0. The project's own lattice-eft-equivalence.cabal declares only `ghc-options: -Wall` for both components (not -Wextra -Werror), so the code meets its own stated build configuration; the stricter three-flag bar was requested by the review brief, not claimed by the paper or repository. Severity is low (test-only hygiene, trivially fixed with a `{-# OPTIONS_GHC -Wno-orphans #-}` pragma or by relocating the instances) and does not affect the correctness of anything the properties demonstrate.

cabal.project pins `with-compiler: ghc-9.10.1`. On this machine that version is installed only via ghcup and is not on PATH by default. I verified directly that a `cabal build` invoked without first adding ghcup's bin directory to PATH fails immediately with 'Cannot find the program ghc' rather than silently falling back to the ambient GHC 9.14.1. This is standard Haskell toolchain-pinning behavior and trivially fixed (`ghcup install ghc 9.10.1`, or editing/removing the with-compiler line), but it is undocumented in the repository (no README or build instructions inside src/lattice-eft-equivalence/), so a reproducer without ghcup already configured for 9.10.1 will hit an opaque error on the first attempt. Separately, cabal prints a cosmetic warning when using ghc-9.10.1 ('Unknown/unsupported ghc version detected: Cabal 3.10.3.0 supports ghc version < 9.10'); this does not affect build or test outcomes.

No dependency freeze file (no cabal.project.freeze) and no version bounds beyond `base >=4 && <5` for `containers` or `QuickCheck` in lattice-eft-equivalence.cabal, so exact resolved package versions are not pinned or recorded anywhere in the repository. My build resolved both from the local package index/store with no visible network fetch, so I cannot independently confirm which QuickCheck version a from-scratch clone on a different machine would resolve to, though the properties used (Arbitrary, Gen, choose, ==>, quickCheckResult) are long-stable QuickCheck API unlikely to break across recent major versions.

data_availability is marked unspecified because this paper is a purely constructive/algebraic verification (finite monoid presentations and a finite periodic table) with no empirical dataset; 'data availability' does not meaningfully apply to its content, and no dataset claim appears anywhere in the text.

No external repository URL, DOI, or Zenodo-style archive appears anywhere in the manuscript or bibliography. Section 6 does give the correct in-repo relative path (`src/lattice-eft-equivalence/`) and every source file carries an explicit MIT license header, which is why code_availability is recorded as open_source rather than unspecified, but a reader holding only the PDF, without the shared project checkout, has no way to fetch the code.
